title: South Indian Coconut-Based Gravy
description: South Indian coconut-based gravy that works beautifully as a base or accompaniment for Fish Curry, Vegetable Stew, and Prawns. This gravy is creamy, aromatic, and balanced with traditional spices.

ingredients.title: Ingredients
items: Fresh grated coconut — 1 cup (or frozen, thawed)
Dried red chilies — 2 (optional, for extra heat)

instruction.title: Step 1: Prepare the Coconut Paste
steps: In a blender, combine grated coconut, green chilies, ginger, garlic, and shallots.
Add about ½ cup of water and grind to a smooth paste. Add more water if needed to get a thick but smooth consistency.

instruction.title: Step 2: Tempering
steps: Heat coconut oil in a pan over medium heat.
Add mustard seeds and let them splutter.
Add fenugreek seeds, dried red chilies, and curry leaves. Sauté for 30 seconds until fragrant.

instruction.title: Step 3: Cook the Paste
steps: Add the ground coconut paste to the pan.
Stir well and cook on medium-low heat for about 5-7 minutes, stirring frequently to avoid sticking.

instruction.title: Step 4: Add Spices and Seasoning
steps: Mix in turmeric powder, coriander powder, and black pepper.
Add tamarind pulp if using, along with salt.
Add remaining water (up to ½ cup) to adjust the gravy consistency.
Simmer for another 5-10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the raw smell disappears and the gravy thickens slightly.

Serving Suggestions: Use this gravy as a base for fish or prawn curry by adding your choice of seafood at the simmering stage.
For a vegetable stew, add mixed vegetables like carrots, beans, potatoes, and peas after the paste is cooked and simmer until tender.
Serve hot with steamed rice, appam, or idiyappam.

chefTips: Use fresh grated coconut for the best flavor and creamy texture.
Cook the coconut paste over medium-low heat to prevent it from sticking or separating.
Adjust the amount of tamarind depending on the dish you are preparing.
Coconut oil gives the gravy its authentic South Indian aroma.
This gravy can be used as a base for fish, prawns, vegetables, or even eggs.
